content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
{916) 322-5660 • Fax {916) 322-0886 
Steven D. Miller 
Partner 
Hanson Bridgett LLP 
425 Market Street, 26th Floor 
San Francisco, CA 94105 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-229 
Dear Mr. Miller: 
December 18, 2015 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of District President Tomas Mattusch for 
advice regarding provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act")1 and Section 1090. Please note 
that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law 
conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
For advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts 
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Mateo County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section I 090, 
the advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding brought against any individual other than the 
requestor." (Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the San Mateo County Harbor District from renewing a 
Commercial Activity Pennit for District President Tomas Mattusch? 
CONCLUSION 
Based upon the facts provided, the Commercial Activity Pennit is a business license that is 
regulatory in nature and not a contract. Accordingly, Section 1090 does not preclude the San Mateo 
Harbor District from renewing the pennit. We caution, however, that District President Mattusch 
may not take part in the decision under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the PRA and must avail 
himself of the same procedure available to the public in submitting his application for renewal. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-15-229 
Page No. 2 
You are the General Counsel for the San Mateo County Harbor District (the "District"). 
Pursuant to Section 1097 .1, and under the authority of the District's President of the Board of 
Harbor Commissioners and its General Manager, you are requesting advice regarding the potential 
application of Section 1090 to the District's President of the Board of Harbor Commissioners. 
The District owns and operates a number of facilities including Pillar Point Harbor in Half 
Moon Bay. A five-member elected Board of Harbor Commissioners (the "Board") governs the 
District. The President of the Board, Tomas Mattusch, took office on January 5, 2015. 
For Pillar Point Harbor, the following District Ordinances apply: 
• Ordinance 5.10.01 0.C requires that anyone using a vessel for commercial purposes within 
the harbor must obtain a Commercial Activity Permit from the District. 
• Ordinance 5.10.0 l 0.E sets numerical limits on the number of Commercial Activity Permits 
the Board will ordinarily issue at any one time.2 
• Ordinance 5.10.0 l 0.F .1 provides that the Board initially approve Commercial Activity 
Permits, which are valid for one year. 
• Ordinance 5.10.010.F. l provides that the General Manager may renew Commercial Activity 
Permits without the need for Board action. 
For over a decade prior to his election, District President Mattusch has operated a charter 
boat service based at Pillar Point Harbor. He berths his boat at Pillar Point Harbor and pays a 
monthly berthing fee according to the District's rate schedule previously established and 
administered uniformly to all members of the public. In 1999, District President Mattusch applied 
for and obtained a Commercial Activity Permit to operate his business pursuant to the District's 
procedures. Since initial approval, the General Manager has renewed District President Mattusch•s 
Commercial Activity Permit each year by administrative action, most recently on December 5, 2014 
(the "Permit"), prior to President Mattusch taking office. The Permit is valid until December 31, 
2015. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Generally, Section 1090 prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Under this section, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig 
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call 
2 Under this ordinance, "The District may not issue commercial activity pennits for more than sixteen ( 16) 
inspected small passenger vessels and six (6) uninspected small passenger vessels at Pillar Point Harbor without first 
detennining the impact of said additional pennits on the Districts facilities and the surrounding community." 


File No. A-15-229 
Page No. 3 
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies regardless of whether the tenns of the contract 
are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.) 
In this instance, it is not contested that District President Mattusch is a public officer subject 
to the provisions of Section 1090. Moreover, as a member of the District's governing board, District 
President Mattusch is presumed to be involved in the making of all contracts by the District 
irrespective of whether he actually participates in making of the contract. (Thomson v. Call, supra, 
at pp. 645, 649.) Accordingly, the detenninative question in this instance is whether the 
Commercial Activity Pennit constitutes a contract for purposes of Section 1090. 
Generally, one may look to general principles of contract law to detennine whether a 
contract is involved in the decision (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 
234 (1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract."' (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 
569; 571.) More specifically, in interpreting the definition of contract for purposes of Section 1090, 
the Attorney General's office has opined that a decision amending a "license" or "regulatory 
pennit" was a "regulatory matter and not a 'contract' in the ordinary sense of the word." (84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen., supra, at p. 36.) Accordingly, a license that is regulatory in nature is not a 
contract for purposes of Section 1090. 
In this case, the District owns and operates Pillar Point Harbor in Half Moon Bay, and has 
regulatory authority over the use of the harbor. In exercising this authority, District Ordinances 
require anyone using a vessel in the harbor for commercial purposes to obtain a Commercial 
Activity Pennit, limit the number of pennits the District ordinarily grants at any one time, and 
establish the requirements for applying for a permit and the District's approval of pennits. Based 
upon these facts, the Commercial Activity Pennit is a license that is regulatory in nature and not a 
contract for purposes of Section l 090. 
The Act 
Nonetheless, we must caution that District President Mattusch may not take part in any 
decision regarding his Commercial Activity Pennit under the provisions of the Act. Pursuant to 
Section 87100, a governmental official may not make, participate in making, or use his or her 
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Under Section 87103(a) and (d), an official has an interest in any business entity in which 
the official has an investment of $2,000 or more or in which the official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or manager. An official also has an interest in any source of income 
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 
87103(c).) Based upon the facts provided, District President Mattusch has interests in his charter 
boat service as a business entity and a source of income. 3 
3 We note that District President Mattusch also has a potential interest in any customer of his business as a 
source of income. (Section 82030.) However, as we have already detennined that District President Mattusch's interests 
in his business preclude him from taking part in decisions regarding his pennit under the Act, it is unnecessary to 
consider any potential effect on customers of the business. 


File No. A-15-229 
Page No. 4 
Considering District President Mattusch' s interests in his business, a conflict of interest will 
arise when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on the interests is 
material. (Regulation 18700.) 
Foreseeability: Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the 
official's agency and a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest. (Regulation 18701(a).) 
Materiality: Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. For District 
President Mattusch's interests, the applicable standard is provided in Regulation 18702.l(a)(5), 
which states that an effect on a business entity is material whenever the business "[a]pplies for a 
permit, license, grant, tax credit, exception, variance, or other entitlement that the official's agency 
is authorized to issue." 
In this case, District President Mattusch's interests in his business are explicitly involved in 
any decision involving the permit. Accordingly, the effect of any decision on District President 
Mattusch's interest is both foreseeable and material, and he may not make, participate in making, or 
use his position to influence the decision.4 
Generally, District President Mattusch may not appear before or communicate with the 
Board regarding the permit except as permitted under Regulation 18704(d)(2). 5 Moreover, District 
President Mattusch may not contact or appear before agency staff regarding the permit except as 
necessary to apply for the permit and to provide staff with any necessary information to process the 
application. (See Sipes Advice Letter, No. A-09-124, an official is not making, participating in 
making, or influencing a governmental decision in submitting a request and providing necessary 
information as required for processing the request so long as the official avails himself of the same 
procedure typically available to any member of the public and is not granted special access to city 
officials or employees.) 
4 Note that Regulation 18704 defines "making, participating in making, influencing a decision." 
5 Regulation 18704(d)(2) provides a limited exception allowing an official to appear at a public meeting of the 
governing board, as a member of the public, to address a matter related solely to his or her interest in a business entity 
owned entirely, or fully directed and controlled, by the official or the official's immediate family. 


File No. A-15-229 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the San Mateo County Harbor District from renewing a 
Commercial Activity Pennit for District President Tomas Mattusch?
sections.conclusion:
Based upon the facts provided, the Commercial Activity Pennit is a business license that is 
regulatory in nature and not a contract. Accordingly, Section 1090 does not preclude the San Mateo 
Harbor District from renewing the pennit. We caution, however, that District President Mattusch 
may not take part in the decision under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the PRA and must avail 
himself of the same procedure available to the public in submitting his application for renewal.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 1090 prohibit the San Mateo County Harbor District from renewing a 
Commercial Activity Pennit for District President Tomas Mattusch?

CONCLUSION: Based upon the facts provided, the Commercial Activity Pennit is a business license that is 
regulatory in nature and not a contract. Accordingly, Section 1090 does not preclude the San Mateo 
Harbor District from renewing the pennit. We caution, however, that District President Mattusch 
may not take part in the decision under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the PRA and must avail 
himself of the same procedure available to the public in submitting his application for renewal.
